event: Early hominins inhabit the Lake Turkana basin, where some of the oldest known stone tools and fossil ancestors of humans are later discovered.
Homo erectus, including the famous 'Turkana Boy' skeleton found at Nariokotome, lives along the shores of Lake Turkana.
Cushitic-speaking pastoralists migrate into northern Kenya from the Ethiopian highlands, herding livestock across the savanna.
Nilotic and Bantu-speaking peoples begin migrating into the region, gradually introducing ironworking and farming.
The Periplus of the Erythraean Sea, a Greco-Roman trade guide, describes the East African coast and its commerce in ivory and other goods.
Swahili coastal settlements such as those near Lamu and Malindi develop as Indian Ocean trading hubs linking Africa, Arabia, and India.
Bantu-speaking peoples expand widely across the highlands and Rift Valley, forming the ancestral communities of Kikuyu, Luhya, and others.
Traveler Ibn Battuta visits the prosperous Swahili coastal towns of Mombasa and the region, noting their Islamic culture and trade.
Portuguese explorer Vasco da Gama anchors at Malindi, where he secures a pilot to guide him across the Indian Ocean to India.
The Portuguese build Fort Jesus at Mombasa to control the strategic coast and the Indian Ocean trade routes.
Omani Arab forces capture Fort Jesus after a long siege, ending Portuguese dominance over the Kenyan coast.
Sultan Seyyid Said of Oman moves his capital to Zanzibar, strengthening Omani control over the Swahili coast and its ivory and slave trade.
The Berlin Conference and the Scramble for Africa set the stage for European partition, assigning the interior to British spheres of influence.
The Imperial British East Africa Company receives a royal charter to administer and develop trade in the region.
Britain declares the East Africa Protectorate, formalizing colonial rule over the territory that becomes Kenya.
Construction of the Uganda Railway begins at Mombasa, a project that reshapes the region and brings in Indian laborers.
The man-eating lions of Tsavo kill numerous railway workers, halting construction until the lions are finally shot.
The Uganda Railway reaches Lake Victoria, opening the interior highlands to European settlement and commerce.
Britain opens the fertile 'White Highlands' to European settlers, displacing African communities from their land.
Nairobi becomes the capital of the East Africa Protectorate, growing from a railway depot into a colonial administrative center.
The territory is reorganized as Kenya Colony, a British Crown colony, intensifying settler control and African grievances.
Harry Thuku founds the Young Kikuyu Association, an early organized African protest against colonial land and labor policies.
The Kenya African Union is formed to advance African political rights, later led by Jomo Kenyatta.
The Mau Mau uprising erupts against British colonial rule, prompting the colonial government to declare a state of emergency.
Jomo Kenyatta is convicted of managing the Mau Mau movement and imprisoned by the colonial authorities.
The Mau Mau rebellion is largely suppressed after the capture and execution of field leader Dedan Kimathi.
The state of emergency is lifted and the Kenya African National Union (KANU) is founded as the main nationalist party.
Kenya gains independence from Britain on 12 December with Jomo Kenyatta as its first prime minister.
Kenya becomes a republic with Jomo Kenyatta as its first president, and joins the United Nations.
The assassination of cabinet minister Tom Mboya sparks ethnic tensions, and the opposition KPU party is banned, making Kenya a de facto one-party state.
Jomo Kenyatta dies in office and Vice President Daniel arap Moi succeeds him as president.
Kenya is declared a one-party state by law, and a failed air force coup attempt against President Moi is crushed.
Under domestic and international pressure, the constitution is amended to permit multiparty politics.
Kenya holds its first multiparty elections in decades, which President Moi wins amid opposition divisions and allegations of fraud.
Al-Qaeda bombs the U.S. Embassy in Nairobi, killing more than 200 people in a coordinated terrorist attack.
Mwai Kibaki wins the presidency, ending KANU's nearly four-decade hold on power and Moi's 24-year rule.
Kenyan environmentalist Wangari Maathai wins the Nobel Peace Prize, the first African woman to receive the honor.
A disputed presidential election triggers widespread post-election violence that kills over 1,000 people and displaces hundreds of thousands.
A power-sharing agreement brokered by Kofi Annan ends the post-election crisis, creating a coalition government and the post of prime minister.
Kenyans approve a new constitution by referendum, devolving power to counties and strengthening checks on the presidency.
Uhuru Kenyatta is elected president, and al-Shabaab militants attack Nairobi's Westgate Mall, killing dozens.
Al-Shabaab gunmen massacre nearly 150 people, mostly students, at Garissa University College in northeastern Kenya.
The Supreme Court annuls the presidential election over irregularities, ordering a rerun that Uhuru Kenyatta wins amid an opposition boycott.
William Ruto wins a closely contested presidential election, succeeding Uhuru Kenyatta after a court upholds the result.
Mass youth-led protests against a controversial finance bill force the government to withdraw the proposed tax increases.
